And so, again, just like having everyone that's spoken so far come up here and share, like it's like Christmas to me. I can't wait to sit down with my notebook and take notes. I have some notes for you guys from my stuff. I'll tell you about it afterwards. If you just go to those links, you'll find the stuff. This, Michael did bring some of. I definitely suggest you guys should pick them up. I think the DVDs are like $50, and the CDs are like $20, $25. He's got some. You'll see. This is just really good stuff. I'm excited to have him. And the stage is yours, sir. Thank you so much. Thank you. Thank you so much. I haven't done anything yet, so no applause yet. Thanks. First, I really want to thank Russell and Dagen for inviting me. I mean, if you could shut the project off, we're not going to be looking at that anymore. As I think he said, or if you don't know, I'm basically a Hollywood guy. I have spent the last 30 years plus working in Hollywood always with screenplays, always with writers, producers, stars, directors of screenplays, working with people to help them get their script or their project to a point where it's either they're able to sell it. If it's a screenwriter trying to sell their script, or if it's in pre-production, working with them to get it ready to go into production, so the story works well. And I have been lecturing almost that long about the same thing. But usually, I'm talking to screenwriters or groups of fiction writers in other areas, novelists, and so on. So this is a whole new world for me, and I'm very excited. I'm honored that they asked me here, since I don't have the slightest idea what you've been talking about for the last two days. I'm still trying to figure out how you send email, blind carbon copies. But anyway, we won't go there because I'm sort of a one-trick pony. Because what I promise in the next two hours is you are going to know how to write better stories. I promise you'll be able to do that. I'm also, if I really do my job, I'm going to show you how stories... It's not the principle of story that just applies to how you write, but using the principles of story, I think, will help you greatly in understanding your buyers, your clients, your team, the people you work with, and the people you're marketing to. And if I really do my job, I think maybe I'll even show you how story can help you by applying it to your own life. And if I don't do any of those things, I will say, if you stick around to the end, at least I have a surprise for everybody. Okay? Because I am a whore, and I want you to like me for some reason, or they won't invite me back. And I should say, by the way, that this surprise that you're getting cost me 10 cents apiece. Okay? In internet terms, that means it's worth $99.95 as far as I can tell from what I've learned so far. But it's like street value on drugs, you know? It's like $3 billion worth of marijuana was found. Okay. So anyway, since I am new and don't know any of you, and I know a lot of you know each other, but it may not everybody, I thought a good way to start would just be if we could quickly go around the room, and if each person could just stand up, give us a, I'm not really going to do that. I just love seeing the expression as you're all wishing you were sitting on the aisle seats, you know? Okay. So I'm really, this whole two hours in essence is going to be to teach you one thing. Because as storytellers, in whatever form, whether you're doing an email, or a screenplay, or a novel, or play, or selling tool, whatever, you must have one primary objective. I know Dagen talks a lot about how stories lead to belief. And I think that's a great concept. I never quite thought about it that way. But I would maintain that's true. But before a story can lead to changing or creating a belief, it's got to do something else. In other words, there is one thing you must have as your primary goal whenever you're writing a story. And that is you must elicit emotion. People do not watch movies. They don't buy books. They don't go to plays. So they can observe the people on the screen laughing, crying, getting turned on, or getting frightened. They go so they can have those experiences themselves. And so what you must do with your story is create an emotional experience for the reader, or the audience, or whoever it is, if it's a movie, the audience. But we'll just stay with readers because that's really the level at which we're talking about reaching them. Now, when it comes to achieving that goal of eliciting emotion, to me, stories are simple. Stories are simple. And the principles of story are by and large very simple, too. I have worked with brand new screenwriters, worked writing their first script, and novelists trying to break in. And I've worked with bestselling authors, screenwriters paid a million dollars for their script, movies that are about to go into production. And there's hardly ever a situation when I'm coaching any one of those people that at some point I don't say, this is too complicated. And especially because I'm mostly working with movies. And movie stories are very simple. Now, movies can have layers of complexity. Inception, that Russell mentioned. That's complex, but at its core, it's a simple story. A guy leads a group of people to invade a guy's mind to change his mind about something. So, the first way to understand the simplicity of story is to understand that stories are really built on a foundation of three basic elements. These are the three elements that must be present in any story. And those elements are character, desire, and conflict. Conflict. Character, desire, conflict. Once upon a time there was a girl named Red Riding Hood. She wanted to take a basket of goodies to her grandmother who lived in the woods. What she didn't know is that the big bad wolf was waiting to gobble her up. I'm just realizing the poor person who's filming me, you should be prepared. I like to pace. I know she was hoping to sit back and just have a stationary speaker, but I'm like a bear in one of those arcade games. I just pace around. It's just sort of what I do. So she's going to be flipping that camera the whole time. Okay. Once there was a girl named Red Riding Hood, wanted to take a basket of goodies to her grandmother who lived in the woods. She didn't know the big bad wolf was waiting to gobble her up. Character, Red Riding Hood, desire, basket of goodies to grandma, conflict, big bad wolf. And those are the elements that constitute every novel, every movie, every play, every episode of any TV series, every epic poem, every opera, every short story of a fairy tale. Any kind of fictional story is built on those three elements. Now, as I said, I'm really a Hollywood guy. Anything I know about story really starts with and comes primarily from me working on screenplays. And before that, just watching lots and lots of movies. I mean, I actually moved to Los Angeles so I could break into the movie business, having no idea what I'd do, and then I got on a track of reading scripts and giving comments and then sort of went from there. So since I was five years old, my dad actually made and sold popcorn to the movie theaters where I grew up. I've loved movies. So as a Hollywood guy, and because if your goal is to elicit emotion, the masters of eliciting emotion are in Hollywood. The Hollywood, the mainstream movie business creates more emotional experience globally than any other art form, any other form really, around the world making billions of dollars and so on. So for our purposes, I'm going to focus on movies because I think they're even more specifically instructional for what you want to do. And I think you'll agree with me by the time I'm done at any rate. So that foundation of character, desire, and conflict, in Hollywood, it's a little more specific. The three elements at the foundation of any mainstream movie are hero, outer motivation, outer conflict. Hero, outer motivation, outer conflict. So what do I mean by that? Now, I should prepare you for two things. One, a lot of this jargon I'm using, like when I say hero or other terms, they're just mine. This isn't like websters or other speakers or writers or whatever. So I just want to use the terms so we're all on the same page. It's not important that you know the exact term in other contexts, just so you'll know what I'm talking about. And what I mean by hero is simply the protagonist of the story. It's the main character. It's either Bertie and the King's speech, or you could argue there's two heroes in Wedding Crashers, both the Vince Flandre and the Owen Wilson character. It's the person whose desire drives the story forward. It's the person in a movie that's on screen most of the time. I just use the term hero because it's simple. But understand to me a hero, and this would apply to me in any, even if you use the Chris Vogler, who the video that Deggan mentioned that he listened to or the audio he listened to, the seminar I did was with Chris Vogler, who wrote the writer's journey that John mentioned. There you are. Yeah, I mentioned last night. Great book. I agree completely, and he's a longtime friend, so we did that together. But even if you take the traditional Joseph Campbell approach to hero, to me, a hero is not someone who is heroic. A hero is someone who is given the opportunity to be heroic. And the hero's journey or the story is going to be about what happens to that hero and what will he do with that opportunity. So, main character in Hollywood, it's the hero. Any successful movie is going to have at least one. You could have, like in Wedding Crashers, you could have two heroes. Or you can even have multiple heroes, although it's fairly rare in something like Big Chill or St. Elmo's Far. I've got to go back quite a ways to think of a multiple hero story, some are traveling pants, whatever. Okay, so we'll just focus on a single hero. Now, by outer motivation, I mean that that desire that is forming the foundation of the story, that desire the hero is pursuing must be visible. Outwardly visible. In other words, if you have a hero whose desire, let's say you, I ask you what's your story about or what's a movie about. You say it's about a woman who wants to be successful. Okay, that's not a movie. That might be true for whatever you could take a movie like Working Girl. Yes, she wants to be successful. But if you say that, so what? I have no idea what I'm going to see. An outer motivation is one that when you hear what it is, you immediately get a picture of what achieving that goal would look like on the screen. And equally important, your vision will be the same as everybody else's who say that. So if you ask me what your story about, and I said it's about a high school girl who wants to be accepted. Again, I don't know what you'd picture. High school girl, you have no idea what's going to happen. I said it's about a high school girl who wants to win the statewide cheerleading championship. Now, all of you just got a picture of some big competition where she's jumping around and possibly winning. Now, she may be doing that to find herself or be accepted or whatever, but the outer motivation is the visible goal. And trust me on this, this is one of those concepts that is simple but not easy. At least when I'm dealing with writers, it's really hard, even though they all understand the words I just said, you understand it. But we don't generally think in those terms when we're talking about story. Because that goal is really not what draws us to stories. It's the emotion and it's how the character changes and the inner life and the theme and what it's saying and all the texture and so on. But none of that stuff is going to happen unless you have that clear, visible finish line. I said I was going to warn you about a couple of things. The other thing I should warn you is I love to talk in absolutes. So when I say it must be this way or movies do this, just translate that to mean 90% of the time. Okay, and then I don't have to keep saying most of the time because you know, it gives me more of a sense of authority if I can talk in absolutes. So writers often have a tough time grasping this. And I would say in thinking about what you're going to be doing with the stories you're working with, my suggestion would be to latch on to that principle. If you're telling a success story, which are a lot of the stories that you know, you've been talking about either your own, like John was talking about his way to set yourself up as the Superman and the likeable character. Was that the term the likeable character? Attractive character. Yeah, I was sort of disappointed because I think I could get people to like me, but I'm never going to be that attractive. So anyway, I digress. I digress shouldn't criticize the other speakers for not catering to me. Okay, so a lot of the stories that I'm you're talking about are those your own autobiography about how you got to where you were, or if you're talking about somebody else who you helped or whatever. The story will be more compelling if you give that character a specific goal. If you don't just say, I was down and out and poor, and I really wanted to be successful, or I really wanted to be rich, or I really wanted to be able to pay the bills. To me, if you're telling a story about someone who entered your world or started doing this, and you portrayed this person as in bad shape, down and out, and said, but all she really wanted to do was make $5,000 so she could send her son to college. To me, that's a more compelling story because the more specific you are about where that character wants to get at the end, the more we're going to stay focused and say, but did she get it? Did she get it? And we can track that through the course of the story. Okay, and that's what Hollywood Moves are about here. What are these two movies that I showed clips about? We've got Birdie, the hero of the King's Speech, and a lot of people might say that's a biography, but it's not. A biography is where you tell everything that happens through the course of a person's life. Hardly ever do those movies make money. They usually tank, like Amelia or Hoffa or what was the most recent, there was one biography. But the reason they do it is there's no goal. It's just, biographies are not really structured. It's because real life isn't really structured. I mean, in real life, basically a lot of shit happens and then you die. And that's not really going to pull a lot of people into the theater. So what the writer did so brilliantly is we pretty much know everything we probably need to know about the future of King George. But it's all within the package of it's about a guy who wants to give a speech without stuttering. It's all about the King's, it's all about that double entendre, the King's Speech. Can he give the speech? Will he give the speech? And in Wedding Crashers, it will be, it really focuses a bit more on Owen Wilson, but the key question is, is he going to be able to win the love of the Rachel McAdams character by the end of that movie? That's what, in love stories, there's always an outer motivation because it's always to win the love of the other character. So that outer motivation, that desire is going to be what drives your story forward, that carries your reader to the end of what you want to say. The third thing is the outer conflict. That means the obstacles that character faces must also be visible. It can't just be, but they were afraid and they were lazy and they were, you know, whatever those inner conflicts or tugs of war or weaknesses or whatever, it can't just be that, that's not going to hold your audience. You're going to have to just translate audience to mean reader and back and forth because it's the same thing. Now, there must be obstacles your character faces that are visible obstacles, whether they're either forces of nature or other characters. Forces of nature could be, you know, there's a disaster, they're in a flood or whatever. There could be a disease that they face. In movies, it could be aliens or the shark and jaws or whatever. It's whatever is not a character. But in all stories, there have to be other characters that stand in the hero's way. And here is maybe, I talk in absolutes, I'll probably say about five different things are the most important thing. But the first thing I'm going to say is the most important thing to understand about storytelling is emotion. Remember your job is to elicit emotion. Emotion grows out of conflict, not desire. Sure, you know, your customers, your buyers, whatever the term you want to use is, yeah, they want to make the million bucks. But the story is not about the million bucks. That's not the emotional part. That's just the goal. The story is about all the obstacles they had to overcome. I mean, when Dagen talked about this, look at what he's doing here. High drama, big obstacle. Then we go here. Wait, the hit. No, where did you put? You have this. He immediately wants to deal with the objections. So whatever those are, those are the obstacles that the character is going to have to overcome. And then I think there's one more. And then, oh, the mistakes, obstacles again. So it's the stammer that he has. In wedding crashers, it's the fact that they're lying about who they are. Because all he wanted to do is all they want to do is get laid. And now they have to, you know, over, they have to somehow overcome the obstacle. And there's also another guy who's engaged to her, who's a jerk, and he's trying to expose them for who they are, those visible obstacles. It's the conflicting your story that it was. It's the emotional. So that way of looking at story. That it's about a hero pursuing a clear, visible outer motivation and having to overcome visible obstacles. That is one way of approaching story. That form of story is what I term the hero's outer journey, because it's outwardly visible. We see the hero. We see the steps they take. We envision the goal that they're going after. We have to see the visible obstacles. But there's another way of looking at story that is invisible. If the outer journey is a journey of accomplishment, which it is, in a story that has an outer motivation, what the hero always wants to be able to say at the end is, I did it. I won, or I stopped the bad guy, or I escaped from the villain. I won the love of the guy, whatever it is. The hero wants to be able to say, I did this thing that nobody else did. So it's a journey of accomplishment. But there's also a hero's inner journey. And if the outer journey is a journey of accomplishment, the inner journey is a journey of transformation. More specifically, it is a transformation from fear to courage. It is a transformation of the character from living in fear to living courageously. One of the things, I choose these two clips, partly because two movies I love, and partly because, I mean, if you admit it, when the lights came on, there was a moment when you forgot you were here. And then the second moment you thought, what happens next? You didn't want the lights to go up because that's the emotional involvement you have. Because you've become part of that story. And I picked these two also because they couldn't be more, I mean, one is a broad, R-rated, sort of sexy comedy adult, whatever you'd call it. Wedding Crashers, the other is a true story period piece as far from what Hollywood calls a high concept as you could get. And I swear, everything I talk about today, both of these movies do exactly the same way. The principles are the same regardless. So in the first one, those guys are living in some kind of fear. We'll talk about what it is a little later. And in the second one, obviously, he's terrified just getting up and making the speech. And we will learn in the course of that movie, there's an even bigger fear that he's facing that has to do with becoming king and his place in the world and in his family and so on. So what we're going to do, what I'm going to do for the next hour and 45 minutes is, first I'm going to take you through the outer journey because you have to understand that. If you were movie writers, I'd say you can write a movie that is emotionally involving that's just outer journey. For the life of me, I can't see any sort of transformation or arc for Indiana Jones in Raiders the Lost Arc. And I consider it a great movie. The James Bond movies don't really have much of an arc. The bad ones, which was everything after from Russia with Love until Daniel Craig. You know, it's just more shooting. But if you've got enough explosions, if you've got enough big action to elicit emotion, you don't need that other layer. But you can't have a movie that's just inner journey. You can, but then it's an art house film and it'll make $3.50 unless it wins an Oscar and that'll be $4.50. Okay. So I'm going to, first we're going to go through the outer journey, which you must understand. And then I'm going to show you how that inner journey is intertwined with that. And then we're going to, and all through we're going to talk about how this might apply to you. Now, you all have this workbook that should have been on your, everybody should have this. You do kind of have to forgive me. Were there more of these anywhere? There must be chairs that are empty that have one sitting on. If anybody didn't get one. Okay. You need one? Hang on the questions. If you would, I promise all, I open it up for questions at every sort of logical place, but it'll, it'll be easier that way. Cause you're usually the questions that anticipate something I'm just going to say. So hang on, but don't forget and don't let me not call anyone that time comes. Okay. I, first of all, I was going to say I'm sort of old school. I realize I'm handing out papers and I don't use PowerPoint and stuff like that. But the reason this is here is as we go through the outer journey last night when we broke up, I asked Dagan to tell you be thinking about a story that you're working on or you have written or you're thinking about written for whatever context. The reason is as I talk about these elements and go through them, what I would suggest you do is think about your own story and fill in these blanks as you go down the list. So what you should do to start right now is what's the name of the, who's the hero of your story? Just jot down. Now, if you don't have a name, it's a fictional person. That's fine. If you just put, you know, whatever, so you'll know what it is or if it's yourself, just put your own name and so on. Okay. That's all you can do right now. We're going to get to the others in a second. Now, if you will turn to the next page in the workbook, we have something to excite all of the left brain people in the group and that is a chart. I'm big on left brain charts myself. This is what I call, this is my approach to structure or plot structure. Now, all structure means is the sequence of events. Structure, I don't know if this occurs in other situations where you hear about story, but in Hollywood is sometimes presented as this mystifying, you know, sort of magical set of secrets that are hard to decipher. So, you know, Dave, and you were sort of saying that when you used to hear about the hero's journey and stuff like that. It's not, it's not that complicated. It's really pretty simple and straightforward because all it means is what happens next. It's just the sequence of events in your story. What happens and when does it happen? So, if you're telling somebody, you know, a story about yourself and when you made your first dollar, then you have to decide where does that go? Are you going to begin the story with how excited you were when the first dollar came or is it going to end there or be somewhere in the middle? Those are just structural decisions. And to me, any properly structured story is going to consist of six basic stages. And those six stages to the story are defined by five key turning points. And one of the reasons I was really pleased to get invited here is because when I first started out and I was, for the first decade or so that I was lecturing and developing my sort of approach and stuff like that, I was looking at movies and I figured, I used to say, all movies are divided in, all well-written movies are divided into six stages. And then I got invited to talk to the National Contreras, the Romance Writers of America. This was a few years ago. I'm going there next week again because I'm sort of a regular speaker now. And I realized, well, actually this applies to novels as well. And I also figured out that it applies to episodes of TV shows. But the really cool moment was when I figured out this applies to any story you have, including real life. We'll come back to that. So any story you're telling in any medium or in any form, it will be strengthened if it hits these five turning points, is divided into these six stages. Stage one is what I call the setup. Oh, by the way, the vertical lines have percentages. That's because I use this chart with screenwriters. And if you watch movies, and part of the fun of this might be, my guess is you won't look at movies quite the same way from now on. But if you're talking about movies, the turning points not only are the same for any movie, they occur at exactly the same moment. Exactly the same place in the movie. 90% of the time, right? Okay. So the reason it's a percentage is if you take a movie like Shrek, which is 90 minutes long, and a movie like Avatar, which is two hours and 45 minutes. The turning point is going to be much later in the longer movie. So you can't make it page numbers or minutes of running time. But what happens 10% of the way through Avatar is exactly what happens 10% of the way through Shrek. And if you question that, if you go read a movie, it can't be a movie that you have to read anything on the screen. Okay, it's got to be a Hollywood movie. The next time you run one, look at the counter on your DVD player, look at the minutes of the running time. And I bet within two minutes, either way, it's going to be 10% when you hit this first turning point and so on and so on. Okay, so the first 10% of any story is what I call the setup. There are several things you must do at the beginning of your story when you're setting up your story. When you're in stage one, the first and most important is you must introduce this to your hero. Now, that may seem obvious, but I encounter a lot of stories where that is, and you can, you can, you'll watch movies, for example, that don't always begin with the hero. These two movies, these two that we used the clips for did, but if you take a movie like Da Vinci Code, it starts out with this albino monk killing somebody in the Louvre, and then we cut to the hero. Or if you still, if you take Mission Impossible, I think it was three, the one with Philip Seymour Hoffman, it starts with him having a gun to Ethan Hawks bride's head. He pulls the trigger bang, and then we go back and see everything that led up to that. So you don't have to open the story with your hero, which is a good sort of trick you might want to employ in some of the stories you tell, because so many stories start that way, it might throw somebody off balance if you start with something that's happening to your hero right somewhere in the middle of the journey, as they face the biggest obstacle they face, and then you go back and say, let's see how that character, that person got to this moment of crisis. Okay, but if you do that, you still have to come back and introduce your hero in that setup. The next thing you must do is you must show that hero living her everyday life. A good story is like a before and after picture, like in a weight loss ad. This is the before, this is the picture, the fat picture, the fat fuzzy wearing an ugly bathing suit picture before this person's going to turn into somebody buffed and beautiful. Okay, we must see the hero living her everyday life, because we are going to contrast that to where she ends up at the end of the story. And also because when you tell a story, your listener, your reader, your audience wants to know that they're getting on this train before it leaves the station. We want to see where this character began. We want to know we're here at the beginning and now we're off to the races. So you must show this character living her everyday life. And the third thing you must do is you must create empathy with the character. Empathy. Empathy. It means I feel what you feel. You heard already because John last night, it was interesting because John was talking about empathy. And the point you were making is you have to show your buyers or your audience that you empathize with them. The point I'm making is you have to get them to empathize with the hero of your story. All empathy means is you're creating a psychological connection between your reader and your hero. Because as Dagen said just a little while ago today, stories are a participatory experience. Just like I said, people don't go to the movies to observe. They go to have those emotions. If your story is working, we become the protagonist of your story. I also refer to this as identifying with the character. It just means if that character is in danger, I'm worried. If that character succeeds, I'm happy. If they fail, I'm sad. Or whatever. Your reader becomes that character. And you must create that necessary empathy. And, as luck would have it, there are three key ways you do that. Three devices you can use to create that empathy. Because there are three main reasons in real life why we empathize with someone. The first is create sympathy for the character. In other words, when you introduce your character, show them as being the victim of some undeserved misfortune. You've heard this device used again and again already this weekend. Because in almost all the stories, the character, especially the autobiographical ones, it's like... In fact, my first exposure to Dagen was... Well, we met and then he sent me a set of videos. And you told the story about... Which you've mentioned here about how you first got into all this. And you started right out by saying you had it. I think you were working at a job and you were wearing a tie. Something I know you don't do very much anymore. And you looked around and said to yourself, this is it. That was designed. It also did other things that John talked about and Dagen's talked about. It created this feeling that he has been where the buyer has been. But I would maintain even more than that what it does is it gets us to empathize with him. Because we feel sorry for someone who is stuck in a job they hate and feels like there's no escaping. Or take these two clips I showed you. King's Speech does this brilliantly. Think about it, you can open your story anywhere they could do it anywhere. But what do they do? They put him in a situation where he is mortified and embarrassed to try and give this speech in front of all these people. The first time he's been on the radio it's a new thing. He's come after his father, the king and his brother, the prince. And now he's stuck and he's scared. We can tell that. And everybody sympathizes and then of course he just starts stammering. I mean it really tears your heart out to watch that. It's uncomfortable and so sad. But part of what that does is it gets us to empathize because we feel sorry for him. And even in wedding crashers. Not the deep dramatic situation like in King's Speech. But one of the reasons we care about these guys is they clearly from the way it's shot and Vince Vaughn is just sitting there like this in the opening shot. They've been stuck with these horrible people for days trying to get them to resolve this divorce. So we sort of feel sorry for them that they're in that situation. Okay, so if you can introduce your hero in a way that shows that they're a victim in some way of undeserved misfortune, that will increase our empathy. The second device for creating empathy is put the character in jeopardy. Because we care about people we worry about. And that's also illustrated in these two. By the way, the more of these, you know, if you can do these two or all three things, then it's even stronger. You're not limited to one or the other. Pile them on if you can. So in the King's Speech, not only is he, do we feel sorry for him, but we're also worried about him. Because all the run up is, don't worry, don't worry, which of course means it's very worrisome. If he's going to do it, I hope he does it, I hope he does it. But if he doesn't, he's going to be so embarrassed, that pulls us inside that character. And again, in even a more subtle way, in wedding crashers, they're actually in jeopardy too. Because jeopardy only means the loss of something that's of vital importance to the character. It could be physical jeopardy, like in the beginning of Raiders of the Lost Ark, or a lot of action movies where the hero is in immediate physical danger, you know, with a rolling boulder or a bad guy coming after them or whatever. But it doesn't have to be that. It could be the loss of a job, loss of a loved one. Again, Dagen used this device when he was telling the story this morning. And that was, he said, I was in my car, my boss didn't say a word, but I could tell he was going to fire me. Okay, immediately we start empathizing because we're worried, uh-oh, what if he gets fired? And then he followed up by saying, and I need a job to pay my bills. Uh-oh, he can't pay his bills. That worry is increasing our empathy. Now this is all subconscious stuff. It's not, it's stuff that if you do it right, they're, in spite of themselves, the readers are being pulled into the story for that reason. And the third way you can create empathy is make your hero likeable. And by that I specifically mean show your hero is a good person. Kind, loving, giving, generous, etc. I mean one of the things you should notice, how many, raise your hand if you've seen Wedding Crashers, the whole movie. Okay, the people on this side of the room have, and these four, these four, they don't go to R rated movies, I can tell. Okay, you should, if you remember the movie, these guys are absolute jerks. I mean these are guys that go into weddings where they haven't been invited just to eat food, get laid, and they give the women, they go to bed with fictitious names so they never have to see them and can never be found again. They're horrible guys. I mean if in real life somebody came out and said, guess what I cried and then I never told her my real name and I scored big and now I don't have to worry about it. You'd think, okay, you know, not so admirable. But we still root for them. We want them to get what they want. Why? Because before any of the flaws were revealed, we empathize with them. And at the beginning they're not shown to be jerks, they're actually likeable guys. They really want these people to get on with their lives and they're really good to each other. You can tell they're really close friends, he spends the night with them because you know, because maybe, I mean when you watch the movie you realize he's probably bullshitting her, but you know, he lost his parents so I spend the night with him and so on. So all that is to make us think, well, hey, these are kind of good guys. And in King's Speech what we see that he's loved by his wife, everybody cares about him. Next scene he goes home and he's good to his kids. So he's a likable, good-hearted guy. And in thinking about this for your purposes, it's like when I gave that example and said, isn't the goal more specific? Because she needs the money, I said, so she can send her son to college. I purposely said it that way, and if she just makes enough money to get a speedboat. I mean you could tell that story, but we're not automatically going to like that person. But this is somebody who just wants the money to do something nice for somebody else. So that gets us into that character more quickly. Okay, so beginning your story, or you start with some outside thing and go back, but in the setup, introduce your hero, create empathy for that character, and you do that by describing or showing them living their everyday life. And then 10% of the way through a movie, or some point early in your story, you must present your character with an opportunity. You must present your hero with an opportunity. Something must happen in the story that has never happened to the character before. And that thing will create in that character a desire. What I call the preliminary goal. Something happens and it creates a desire. So in the King's speech, what happens 10% of the way in is his wife, it really happens to Elizabeth first, and then she tells him, so it's sort of a chain of events there. But it's the introduction of Lionel Logue, the speech therapist. So she knocks on the door because she's desperate to help her husband. And she goes and tells him about Lionel Logue. So what is his immediate goal? It's not to give a speech, it's to check this guy out. So his initial goal, his preliminary goal or desire, is just to see if this guy is somebody he would remotely want to work with. And because that desire is going to take your character into stage two of the story, which is what I call a new situation. And in the new situation, the hero's preliminary goal is usually some version of figuring something out, figuring out what's going on, or getting acclimated to some new situation. But it's a smaller goal that will be supplanted or evolve into something even bigger. So his preliminary goal is to check him out. In wedding crashers, which I must confess, they go to 14%, it's one of the exceptions. Because the crashing of the weddings, that's their everyday life, they extend out a bit longer. And I know the guy who wrote it, I talked to him and he's spoken at my lectures and stuff. But I always maintain they made that a little too long and could have been trimmed. But made a zillion dollars, so who am I? So anyway, the opportunity is when they finished all the wedding crashing, they're done for the season, and then one of them gets the paper and brings it in and says, look at this, the daughter of the secretary of the treasurer is getting married, we've got to crash this wedding. So their preliminary goal is to crash this biggest wedding of the season, which they do, and they go to the wedding and now their goal is, check it out. Mostly, who's right for the picking here? Who do I want to get laid with? Who do I want to get into bed at this wedding and hope they're having crab cakes or whatever it is? It's to figure out what's going on, just like Birdie in King's Beach goes and interviews Lionelow. Or in Titanic, it's when the whistle blows and Rose gets on Titanic and it sets sail. Or in Shrek, it's when his home is invaded by fairy tale creatures, and so he says, you got to all go back where you came from and they say, well, we didn't want to come here and Lord Farquhar made us and he says, well, I'm going to find this Lord Farquhar guy and make him send you back home, and he sets out to find Lord Farquhar. That's his preliminary goal. Notice, now this may not apply, I haven't really thought about it, but as you're watching movies anyway, oftentimes the opportunity, the new situation is a change of geography. Now that's not a rule, that's just something you notice, if you were writing screenplays, I'd say consider that because Shrek leaves to find Lord Farquhar and Rose gets on the ship and they go to some place, it's in Maryland, I think it takes place, so wherever the rich people would have weddings, they go there. In the firm, it's when he takes the job with Van Dene Lambert Lock and the next thing they're doing is driving to Memphis and so on. It's not a rule, you don't have to do that, but it does really symbolize that we're really in a new situation. Okay, so the character is trying to figure out what's going on, what's going on, or get acclimated, and then something else happens to the character that has never happened before that creates an even bigger desire. And this desire is more than just figuring something out, this desire is one that has a clear, visible endpoint. Again, critical structural principle to understand, it is here and only here that your hero will begin pursuing the outer motivation. They don't start going for the main goal that defines the movie, he doesn't start working with Lionel Loga 10%, he doesn't try and win the love of Rachel McAdams at 10%, it's not until in the movie the 25%. And I would suggest this, if you're telling stories about yourself or other people who have availed themselves of the program or the coaching or the product, don't just describe that they were bad and then they started doing what I'm selling. What you want to do is say what happened that got them thinking about it, or how they first heard about it, but then they've got, and this is like real life, nobody's going to just be miserable, see one email and just say, okay, I'm going. They're going to have to ponder it. For your purposes, that's where you could probably introduce a lot of the obstacles that people are putting in their head or a lot of the objections as David calls them, or worries and so on. They're trying to figure out, could I do this, could I do this, should I, a lot of money, but I've got to get out of here, or they have to take some preliminary steps, it's the freebies that they would get from you and all that until they sort of pull the trigger. Okay, so at the 25% mark, that's when your character starts pursuing the visible goal. Then, and in a movie, that's at the one quarter mark. Now, once the character starts pursuing the goal, they move into stage three, what I call progress. Because at this stage of the story, whatever plan the character developed to achieve this goal, it seems to be working. So, what happens in King's Speech is, in stage two, he meets, line alone, tries to figure out, maybe I should check this out, raise your hand if you've seen the King's Speech. All the people that didn't see wedding crashes, right? We got the Sophisticates and we got the Sex Comedy people, you know? Okay, good. So, so... Can you do a spoiler right now? Okay. Can you do a spoiler King's Speech right now? I'm sorry, what? Can you do a spoiler, or do you do a spoiler right now, King's Speech? No, no I was, have you not seen it? I haven't seen it now. Okay, I'll try not to ruin it for you. You know, because you have only had three years to go to the best picture winner, but we don't want to rush you, so... You know? Yeah, he's waiting until it shows up on an airplane he has to be on. Okay. I will try not to spoil anything, because I hate to give away endings of anything. Okay, so in stage two, what he does is he checks out Lionel Logue and then he says, forget this, I'm not working with this guy. He makes me come to his house, he's impertinent, he's insulting, I don't want anything to do with this. It's what, now I'm, this is just an aside, but those of you who are familiar with Joseph Campbell or Chris Vogler's book, The Writer's Journey and so on, this is the hero refusing the call. Oftentimes in stories, a hero will be given an opportunity and will say, no, I don't want that. And then something will have to happen to make them change their mind, which is, I mean, I don't have time to cover every possible element of storytelling, but it's something that's fun to be aware of or valuable to be aware of, because it strengthens the story, and I would think from your point of view, your story might be stronger if whatever it is you're offering was refused by the person you're talking about, if they said no and then changed their mind. I would think if I'm in the situation hearing that and I know you're trying to sell me something, I would be more interested now. It's like, what, you're selling this and you're saying they said no, what, you know, but I can't say that was certainly, but it seems like it would be a good tool to use. At any rate, he says no, but then what happens? He plays, he tries again with some idiot who tries to put marbles in his mouth and teach him to speak, and then he listens to the recording of him speaking with no stammer for Lionel Loeb when he played that music real loud, and he thinks, gotta do it. And that's when he says, okay, I'm going to work with him. And he begins his goal of being able to speak without stuttering by working with this guy. That's really what that movie's about. So now his plan seems to be working, he goes and they start working together and working together and so on. Or in wedding crashers, what happens is he meets Rachel McAdams and then they get invited. This is the change of plans. This is the second thing that happens. For King's Speech, it's listening to the record. In wedding crashers, what happens is they get invited to the, I don't know if it's an island or whatever, it's another place rich people are, some mansion, some summer home they have or whatever. Okay, that's never happened before either, and that's when Owen Wilson has met Rachel McAdams and think, her, I want her, I like her. So now he's going to go there specifically to win her love. It might start out with getting her into bed, but it becomes more than that. But that's when he actually starts pursuing her. Just like in King's Speech, when he starts working with the Lionelogue, that's when he really starts taking action to achieve this goal. And the plan seems to be working. It's getting more and more and more and more, it's going along. And then your hero will reach what I call the point of no return. The point of no return in a story is just like it is in Aeronautics. That's where I first heard the term. It means like if you're flying a plane from, I suppose, here to Paris, and you developed engine trouble, well, if you're more than halfway there, you're not going to turn around and come back, because that makes no sense. You've passed that point of where it makes sense. So that's the point of no return when you get more than halfway. And smack in the middle of the story, it means the hero is going to do something that represents a much bigger commitment to the goal. This is actually the trickiest one really to explain, because it's a little more internal sometimes, but you'll see it. In wedding crashers, it's a very typical point of no return. And that is, he's been pursuing her, but pretending they're just going to be friends and so on, and they go down to the beach together, and he gives her a kiss, or they kiss each other. If you start paying attention when you see romantic comedies, that is, or any sort of love story, the midpoint is almost always the first time the two people go to bed together, kiss, declare their love, or go on a first official date. In the King's speech, it's the first time he's at the Cue Garden's flower show or something, it's the first time he makes a speech in public with some stammer, but at least. And the point of no return is both a bigger commitment, but what it means is that your hero is burning their bridges behind them. They're burning their bridges behind them, because up to that point in the story, they could have chosen to turn around and go back to the life they were living. But once they cross the point of no return, there's no turning back. They can try, but it'll never be the same. And my favorite point of no returns is in Titanic, because a series of things happen. First, Rose and Jack go make love in the hold of the ship. Again, very typical that that's the first time the two characters go to bed together at the point of no return, at the midpoint. But immediately after that, they go up on deck and she says, I'm getting off the ship, I'm getting off with you. In other words, she's saying, I'm leaving this other guy, this guy I've been a mistress of or whatever, this kept woman, I'm leaving him and I'm declaring my love for you. Because the point of no return is almost always in a movie, a character actually declaring in some way. It's always an action, but it also often includes saying out loud, this is what I want. It's like going public with the desire. And of course, once she does that, she's burned her bridge behind her, or bridges because once she declares her love for him, she can't really go back to being that guy's mistress. I mean, she could try, but she'd be miserable and she'd be with somebody who knew that she'd been sleeping with this other guy and so on, so it really wouldn't work. Okay, but in Titanic, there's a third thing that happens. They make love, they go up on deck, she declares her love for Jack. Anybody want to guess what happens within 10 seconds of her saying that? It's the iceberg. That's what happens when you have sex on a cruise ship, first of all. Because once your hero crosses the point of no return, the outside world is going to start closing in. Now they have entered stage four, what I call complications in higher stakes. It means that two things happen when your hero makes this full commitment. One is, it becomes much more difficult to achieve the goal, and two, it becomes much more important to achieve the goal. Because the hero has put a lot more on the line, more is at stake. At this point, if she now fails to get to New York with Jack, and when he has love, because that's her two goals, now she's, before it was just, he was a guy that she met, and he was nice, and they got to be acquainted, and then, okay, too bad, he drowned, but no harm, no foul. But now, if they don't do that, she loses the love of her life. But the outside world is closing in, it's going to be much more difficult to get off in New York with him and the ship is sinking, and it's not going to make it to New York. In King's speech, what happens? He crosses that point of no return, and then, as he's doing more of this, the other people around him, not his wife, she's totally devoted and loving, but the Archbishop of Canterbury, and his political advisors, and his father, I don't know if he knows about Lionel Loga, I don't think he learns. They start trying to persuade him that this is bad idea, but more than that, what happens is his father dies, is the abdication. His father dies, and his brother abdicates, and now it's like, oh, you know that speech you had to make? Little sidebar, you also have to be King of England, and we are going to enter a war. So, it's like the stakes have just been monumentally raised in that movie. Now, I don't know with certainty, because again, it's not my world, but my suspicion would be, as you're taking your, when you're telling stories about people you've worked with, or yourself, going through the process of becoming successful at internet marketing, whatever the visible goal is that accompanies that, the point of no return is going to be probably where they put the money on the table. Where they, not for the one dollar offer, but where they say, okay, I'm going to do this. Now, that wouldn't have to be it, but that would be a good example of a full commitment. Because now, if everything goes kabluy and they don't do it, they're out a significant amount of money. Believe me, I'm not saying you have to have that as your midpoint, but there has to be something your character does that shows, I'm not on the fence anymore, I'm doing this. So things get tougher and tougher and tougher, until finally, your hero comes to the fourth key turning point, what I call the major setback. At the major setback, something happens that is so awful, destructive, horrible, or the conflicts just become so big, that it seems to your reader like all is lost. In King's Speech, what happens is, I don't remember it's been a while, but plug your ears, the guy who hasn't heard the movie yet, because we're going too far towards the end, I don't want you to know about this key moment. Okay, it's a combination of, I think the first thing, there are sort of two, and I don't remember which is at the three quarter mark, I checked it, but I can't remember now, it's either when Lionelogue tells him, you're not your father, you can do this, and he gets so mad that he would push him that way, he says, I don't want to see you again, and they split up. Then there's a second moment, but I think that's even later, where he's convinced this guy's no good, because he has no credentials. But I think the key point of no return in wedding crashes is simple. It's the same point, I'm sorry, major setback, it's the same major setback as in every Hollywood romantic comedy you will see, 90% of the time. And that is Hollywood romantic comedy, this is an aside, I won't do you any good in your career, but it's interesting and it's fun to know. Hollywood romantic comedies are built on deception, almost without exception, unless you're talking about Woody Allen, or somebody who sort of blazes their own trail, almost without exception, it's somebody who's lying, somebody who's pretending to be someone they're not, whatever. And at the three quarter mark, the truth always comes out, and they always break up. If you're writing, if you were writers and I say, if you're writing a love story, you got to separate the two lovers at the three quarter mark. And that's what happens in wedding crashes. Bradley Cooper, when he was playing costar jerks, blows the whistle on them, she believes... They're going to break up at the three quarter mark in a movie, but that's what I call the major setback, because she finds out the truth, she thinks he was just trying to get laid, they kick him off this property, and we figure there's no way they can get together now, and that's what we've been rooting for. It's interesting, it was interesting listening to Dagen this morning, and I said this to you during the break, because what he is talking about here, in my opinion, this high drama that's going to lead to an epiphany, he's actually starting this story, I think, with the major setback. But then what he's going to do is, he's going to use that moment, what he's doing is he's putting a major setback point at the very beginning of this, actually he's putting a big major setback moment at the opportunity, that will lead to the epiphany that is going to lead to the preliminary goal. Notice that when that happened, what did he say? I heard the word co-op, that's the opportunity. Now what did he say? I started pacing around, pacing around, co-op, co-op, he's not doing anything. He's figuring it out, he's trying to figure it out, I'm telling you. He went through the six stages, I know, he had to, because he pursued a goal and accomplished it, it has to have these six stages. Thank you.
